metadata.summary: Moderated discussion with 6-10 participants exploring attitudes, reactions to concepts, and shared vocabulary. Minimum 2-3 groups per segment; findings are qualitative and directional.

content.title: Focus Groups: Structure, Moderation, and Analysis
content.summary: A focus group is a moderated discussion with 6-10 participants who share characteristics relevant to the research. The group explores attitudes, reactions to concepts, and language around a topic. Run a minimum of 2-3 groups per segment; findings are qualitative and directional, not statistically generalizable. Focus groups surface group dynamics, shared vocabulary, and cross-segment contrasts that individual interviews spread across too many sessions to achieve quickly.

why.p: Focus groups surface group dynamics, shared vocabulary, and cross-segment contrasts that individual interviews spread across too many sessions to achieve quickly. Running 3-4 homogeneous-but-diverse groups (current users, competitor users, non-users) in parallel reveals segment-specific objections and the language people actually use — inputs that feed copy, positioning, and concept prioritization.

list.item: Early concept exploration: diverse reactions to multiple positioning angles before committing to one direction.
Language and terminology discovery: harvest vocabulary users actually use for copy and search synonyms.
Stakeholder buy-in: leadership needs to "watch users" to believe research findings.
Cross-segment comparison: 3-4 groups reveal segment-unique objections and shared concerns.
Usability testing — group dynamics suppress individual struggle; use moderated 1:1 sessions.
Sensitive topics (health, finances, conflict) — participants self-censor in groups.
Behavior measurement — groups capture stated behavior, not actual behavior.
Final go/no-go decisions — groupthink and dominant voices distort signal.
Quantitative claims — N=8 per group is not statistically generalizable.

rule.statement: Use focus groups for attitude exploration, concept reactions, language discovery, and cross-segment comparison. Do not use them for usability testing, sensitive topics, individual behavior measurement, or final go/no-go decisions.
rule.rationale: Group dynamics are a feature for attitude exploration (diverse reactions surface quickly) and a bug for usability testing (participants suppress struggle when observed by peers). The method matches some research goals and actively harms others.

rule.statement: Run minimum 2-3 groups per segment. Never report a finding from a single group.
rule.rationale: A single group finding is anecdote, not pattern. Group composition, moderator dynamics, and random participant selection create too much session-level noise for any single group to be representative.

rule.statement: 6-10 participants per group. Homogeneous enough for comfort (common frame of reference), diverse enough for variety (different perspectives). Run 2-4 groups total.
rule.rationale: Below 6: insufficient dynamic diversity. Above 10: moderator cannot manage all voices; quieter participants get lost. Homogeneity enables candor; diversity enables range.

rule.statement: Screen aggressively for professional respondents (people who cycle across studies). Use live screener calls or short video tasks to verify participation quality.
rule.rationale: Professional respondents give polished, rehearsed answers that do not represent real users. They are common on large panel platforms and inflate perceived consensus.

section.name: discussion-guide-structure
section.p: Standard 90-min structure: Opening 5 min (introductions, ground rules) → Warm-up 10 min (easy questions) → Core topics 40-60 min (3 topics maximum) → Concept reaction 15 min (if showing stimuli) → Wrap-up 5 min (summary, final thoughts).

rule.statement: Show concepts and stimuli only after the warm-up and initial topic exploration. Introducing stimuli too early biases the discussion toward reacting to your framing rather than expressing independent attitudes.
rule.rationale: Pre-stimulus exploration captures the user's unprompted mental model. Introducing stimuli first anchors all subsequent discussion to the product frame, suppressing organic vocabulary and unprompted concerns.

section.reference: Full 90-min discussion guide template with opening script, ground rules, topic sections, concept-reaction block.

section.name: moderation-techniques

rule.statement: Capture written first-impressions (silent post-it round) before group discussion to break anchoring on the first speaker's view. Then open the floor.
rule.rationale: The first speaker's view anchors the whole group. Written capture before discussion preserves independent assessments that would otherwise be lost to social conformity.

section.reference: Note-taking template: participant table, group dynamics, concept reactions, moderator observations.

section.name: post-session-pipeline

rule.statement: Use speaker diarization on all focus group recordings. Without speaker labels, transcript analysis cannot identify dominant-participant skew or per-segment quote attribution.
rule.rationale: Dominant participants can drive 40-60% of transcript volume. Without diarization, LLM theme extraction weights their views as group consensus. Diarization allows per-speaker filtering before analysis.

rule.statement: Run a dissent prompt pass explicitly after the consensus pass: "What disagreement existed within this group?" LLMs are biased toward consensus narratives and collapse nuanced dissent into a single theme without explicit prompting.
rule.rationale: Consensus-biased synthesis misses the finding that often matters most: the minority view, the segment-specific objection, the latent concern. Dissent prompting is the structural counter to this bias.

section.reference: Shell pipeline: ffmpeg audio extract → WhisperX diarize → Claude theme extraction.

rule.statement: Tag transcripts with both speaker ID and segment label before analysis. Filter synthesis prompts by segment to surface cross-group contrasts: shared themes, segment-unique themes, and contradictions.
rule.rationale: Untagged cross-group analysis merges distinct segment signals and produces a false "global user" view. Segment filtering preserves the core value of running multiple groups.

rule.statement: Count unique speakers per theme, not utterance count, to assess theme strength. Polite agreement ("yeah, that's right") is not endorsement.
rule.rationale: High utterance count from a single speaker inflates theme strength artificially. Unique-speaker count is the correct denominator: consensus = most participants expressed the theme independently.

section.name: reporting
section.p: Report structure per finding: theme name → what we heard (summary) → representative verbatim quote with speaker type → implication for design or strategy.

rule.statement: Report per-group findings before cross-group synthesis. Never aggregate groups before documenting group-level patterns.
rule.rationale: Early aggregation destroys segment signals. Cross-group synthesis must build on documented per-group patterns, not replace them.

antipattern.description: Dominant participant drives the transcript and LLM analysis treats their views as group consensus.
antipattern.reason: Without diarization and speaker-filtered analysis, a participant generating 50% of transcript volume gets 50% weight in theme extraction. Always diarize and compute unique-speaker-count per theme before any consensus claims.

antipattern.description: Online groups analyzed without adjusting for the ~30% nonverbal signal loss versus in-person sessions.
antipattern.reason: Zoom groups flatten energy, disagreement, and hesitation. Online findings should be framed as stated attitudes only, not as a proxy for in-person dynamics. Use smaller groups (4-6) and more structured activities to compensate.

antipattern.description: LLM theme extraction over-clusters nuanced disagreement into a single theme without explicit dissent prompting.
antipattern.reason: LLMs trained on consensus-seeking corpora produce consensus-seeking summaries. Force dissent extraction with a separate pass: "What disagreements or tensions existed?" before merging with the consensus pass.

antipattern.description: Auto-generated themes form neat categories without an unclassified bucket.
antipattern.reason: Every real focus group transcript contains messy, boundary-spanning material that resists clean categorization. Forcing 100% classification loses the material that often drives the most interesting insights. Always include a "messy/unclassified" bucket.

antipattern.description: LLM-generated discussion guides over-rely on closed-ended probing questions.
antipattern.reason: Agents produce grammatically correct but logically leading questions by default. Human researchers must rewrite at least 30% of probes for genuine openness before the guide is used.

section.name: agentic-workflow
section.p: Agents operate around the human moderator, not as moderators. Pre-session: a research agent drafts the discussion guide from objectives, generates probing follow-ups, and screens recruits. Post-session: a transcription agent (Whisper + diarization) produces speaker-labeled transcripts; a synthesis agent extracts themes per segment with verbatim quotes; a comparison agent maps cross-group agreement/disagreement. A human signs off each theme before it lands in the report.

list.item: `faion-ux-researcher-agent` — drafts guide, designs probes, performs cross-group synthesis.
`faion-content-marketer` — extracts user-language vocabulary for copy reuse.
`faion-product-manager` — converts findings into prioritized opportunity statements.
`faion-market-researcher` (from `pro/research/market-researcher`) — segments + recruitment screener logic.

section.name: theme-extraction-prompt-pattern
cross-group-comparison-prompt-pattern
cli-tools
section.p: Tools and services for focus group execution and analysis:

list.item: OpenAI Whisper / `whisper-cli`: Transcribe recordings locally. Install: `pip install openai-whisper`.
`pyannote.audio`: Speaker diarization (who said what). Install: `pip install pyannote.audio`.
WhisperX: Whisper + diarization combined. Install: `pip install whisperx`.
`ffmpeg`: Extract audio from Zoom recordings. Distro package.
`yt-dlp`: Download recordings from links if needed. Install: `pip install yt-dlp`.
Anthropic SDK: Theme extraction with prompt caching across long transcripts. Install: `pip install anthropic`.

section.name: saas-platforms
section.p: Services and apps for online focus groups and recruitment:

list.item: Zoom (SaaS, Partial): REST API for cloud recordings. Cheapest for online groups; export MP4+VTT.
UserTesting Live Conversations (SaaS, Partial): API for sessions. Built-in note-taking, recruit panel.
Discuss.io (SaaS, Yes): REST API. Purpose-built for online focus groups.
Recollective (SaaS, Yes): API. Asynchronous + live group hybrid.
dscout (Live) (SaaS, Yes): API. Diary + live group combo.
Respondent.io (SaaS, Yes): API. High-quality recruitment, niche segments.
User Interviews (SaaS, Yes): API. Recruitment panel, screener builder.
Otter.ai / Fireflies (SaaS, Partial): Cloud transcription; check data residency before sending sensitive PII.
Dovetail (SaaS, Yes): API. Tagging + theming workspace; agent-writable via API.
EnjoyHQ / Marvin (SaaS, Yes): Research repository; agent can post quotes/themes.
Run a minimum of 2 groups per segment; never report a finding from a single session.
Brief the moderator separately from the agents — moderators need context-flexibility, not scripted prompts. Agents prepare the guide; humans run the room.
Capture written first-impressions (silent post-it round) before group discussion to break anchoring on the first speaker's view.
Tag transcripts with both speaker ID and segment label; filter analysis prompts by segment to surface cross-group contrasts.
Run a "dissent prompt" pass explicitly: "What disagreement existed within this group?" — counters LLM bias toward consensus narratives.
Compensate participants well ($75-150/90 min in US baseline; higher for B2B/specialist). Lowballing screens for low-quality respondents.
Whisper without diarization assigns all speech to one speaker → quote attribution becomes unreliable. Always run diarization for groups.
Long transcripts (>50k tokens) require chunked summarization; without overlap windows, themes spanning chunk boundaries get lost. Use a 10-15% overlap.
LLMs treat polite agreement ("yeah, that's right") as endorsement. Calibrate by counting unique speakers per theme, not utterance count.
Auto-generated themes invent neat categories; force the model to also output a "messy/unclassified" bucket and review it manually.
Privacy: never upload raw recordings to a third-party LLM endpoint without explicit consent in the participant agreement. Default to local Whisper for sensitive segments.
Recruitment fraud (bots/repeats) is now common via global panels. Verify with a live screener call or short video task before slotting into a group.
LLM-generated discussion guides over-rely on closed-ended phrasing. Always have the human researcher rewrite at least 30% of probes for openness.

template.purpose: Full 90-min discussion guide template with opening script, ground rules, topic sections, concept-reaction block.
Note-taking template: participant table, group dynamics, concept reactions, moderator observations.
Shell pipeline: ffmpeg audio extract → WhisperX diarize → Claude theme extraction.
